request.question: Among film directors, who was more well-known for horror films, Eric Red or Howard Hughes?

title: Summa Corporation
Bangladesh Film Directors Association
Altic/Howard Hughes (METRORail station)
Eric Red
The Tights
The Amazing Howard Hughes
List of Cambodian films of 1966
Howard Hughes Corporation
Hughes XF-11
Howard Hughes
sentences: Summa Corporation was the name adopted for the business interests of Howard Hughes after he sold the tool division of Hughes Tool Company in 1972.
The tool division would merge with Baker International in 1987 to form Baker Hughes, the world's third-largest oil-services company.
The name "Summa", Latin for "highest", was allegedly chosen by several of Hughes' employees without consulting him first.
Hughes was allegedly dissatisfied, and preferred the name "HRH Properties" - with the initials standing for both "Howard Robard Hughes" and "Hughes Resort Hotels".
His suggestions were never implemented, and the company remained Summa for 18 years following Hughes' death.
Bangladesh Film Directors Association is the pan-national trade body of film directors in Bangladesh.
The Bangladesh Film Directors Association’s General Secretary is Badiul Alam Khokon.
Mushfiqur Rahman Gulzar is the president of Bangladesh Film Directors Association.
Altic/Howard Hughes is a light rail station in Houston, Texas on the METRORail system.
It is served by the Green Line and is located on Harrisburg Boulevard at Altic Street in the East End.
The station is named for Altic Street as well as Howard Hughes, who built a Hughes Tool Company factory in the area.

Eric Red (born Eric Joseph Durdaller; February 16, 1961) is an American screenwriter and director, best known for writing the horror films "The Hitcher" and "Near Dark", as well as writing and directing "Cohen and Tate".
The Tights are an English punk rock band from Worcester.
The band's only two singles were released by the Cherry Red Records label: "Bad Hearts" / "It" / "Cracked" (1978) and "Howard Hughes" / "China's Eternal" (1978).
After John Peel played "Bad Hearts" on BBC Radio 1, this single made it to number six in the UK punk chart.
Follow-up "Howard Hughes" reached number four in the same chart.
After playing their farewell gig at the Marquee Club, London, The Tights split in 1979.

The Amazing Howard Hughes is a 1977 American made-for-television biographical film about American aviation pioneer and filmmaker Howard Hughes, based on the book "Howard: The Amazing Mr. Hughes" by Hughes' business partner Noah Dietrich.
The film starred Tommy Lee Jones, Ed Flanders, and Tovah Feldshuh and was made a year after Hughes's death, in 1976, while aboard a private flight to Mexico.
It was originally broadcast in two parts on CBS on April 13 and 14, 1977.

Ros Serey Sothear becomes a popular artist for movie soundtracks among film directors, taking Huoy Mea's old position Of the 18 films listed, 4 films are in existence, 6 have been remade, and 8 have not yet been remade.
The Howard Hughes Corporation () is a major real estate development and management company based in Dallas, Texas.
It began as part of the oil drilling tool business founded by Howard R. Hughes, Sr., in 1913, which under his son Howard Hughes, Jr., diversified into real estate development.
The real estate operations became a separate company, which was acquired by The Rouse Company, which in turn was acquired by General Growth Properties (GGP).
In 2010, GGP formed The Howard Hughes Corporation as a separate company.
The Hughes XF-11 was a prototype military reconnaissance aircraft, designed and flown by Howard Hughes and built by Hughes Aircraft for the United States Army Air Forces.
Although 100 F-11s were ordered in 1943, only two prototypes and a mockup were completed.
During the first XF-11 flight in 1946, Howard Hughes crashed the aircraft in Beverly Hills, California.
The production aircraft had been cancelled in May 1945, but the second prototype was completed and successfully flown in 1947.
The program was extremely controversial from the beginning, leading the U.S. Senate to investigate the F-11 and the Hughes H-4 Hercules flying boat in 1947–1948.

Howard Robard Hughes Jr. (December 24, 1905 – April 5, 1976) was an American businessman, investor, pilot, film director, and philanthropist, known during his lifetime as one of the most financially successful individuals in the world.
He first made a name for himself as a film producer, and then became an influential figure in the aviation industry.

Later in life, he became known for his eccentric behavior and reclusive lifestyle—oddities that were caused in part by a worsening obsessive–compulsive disorder (OCD) and chronic pain from a plane crash.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

response.hop1_query: Among film directors, who was more well-known for horror films, Eric Red or Howard Hughes?
hop1_docs: Eric Red | Eric Red (born Eric Joseph Durdaller; February 16, 1961) is an American screenwriter and director, best known for writing the horror films "The Hitcher" and "Near Dark", as well as writing and directing "Cohen and Tate".

Eric Brian Hughes | Eric Brian Hughes also known as E.B. Hughes, is an American writer, director and producer.  He has written, produced and directed two feature films, "Pacing the Cage" (2014) and "Turnabout" (2016).  His screenplay "The Fallen Faithful" won Best Screenplay at the 2011 Beverly Hills Film Festival.  Hughes is a member of the Producers Guild of America.

Howard Deutch | Howard Deutch (born September 14, 1950) is an American film and television director.  He is perhaps best known for his collaboration with filmmaker John Hughes, having directed two of Hughes' well-received screenplays, "Pretty in Pink" and "Some Kind of Wonderful".  Since 2010, he has primarily directed television productions, including multiple episodes of "Getting On" and "True Blood".

The Creature Walks Among Us | The Creature Walks Among Us, released in 1956, is the third and final installment of the "Creature from the Black Lagoon" horror film series from Universal Pictures, following 1955's "Revenge of the Creature".  The film was directed by John Sherwood, the long-time Universal-International assistant director, in his directorial debut.  Jack Arnold, who had directed the first two films in the series, had moved on to "A-list" films, and felt he had no more to contribute to the horror genre.  He suggested that his assistant director, Sherwood, could move up to full director, which partly affected Universal's decision to allow him to direct the film.  "The Creature Walks Among Us" starred Jeff Morrow, Rex Reason, and Leigh Snowden, and, like the original "Creature from the Black Lagoon", had music composed by Henry Mancini, who at the time was under contract with Universal.

Vernon Zimmerman | Vernon Zimmerman is an American writer and director who made his feature debut as director with the 1962 short "Lemon Hearts" starring Andy Warhol associate Taylor Mead.  Zimmerman wrote and directed the Claudia Jennings roller derby drive-in film "Unholy Rollers" and collaborated with Terrence Malick on the script to the road movie "Deadhead Miles".  He is most well known for his horror slasher film "Fade to Black", a dark and despairing psychological study of an awkward and alienated hardcore film buff who exacts a harsh revenge on his cruel tormentors.  Zimmerman received a Saturn Award nomination as Best Director for the film, a predecessor to more well-known modern parodies of the horror genre.  Vernon also wrote the scripts for the horror-Western "Hex" (a.k.a. "Charmed"), the redneck crime exploitation film "Bobbie Jo and the Outlaw", the made-for-TV wrestling comedy/drama "Mad Bull", the failed TV pilot film "Shooting Stars", and the teen fantasy comedy "Teen Witch".  Zimmerman's latest film is the six-minute comic short "Chuck and Wally on the Road".  More recently Vernon has been working as a script analyst.  He also teaches screen-writing courses at UCLA's Extension and Certificate Program.  Zimmerman also taught classes on both writing feature scripts and directing actors for film and television at the USC School of Cinema and Television.  Vernon Zimmerman lives in Los Angeles and is a member of both the Writers Guild of America and the Directors Guild of America.

Inside (2007 film) | Inside (French: "À l'intérieur" ) is a 2007 French home invasion horror film directed by Julien Maury and Alexandre Bustillo, starring Alysson Paradis and Béatrice Dalle.  It was written by co-director Bustillo, and is the first feature film from either director.  It concerns the attack and home invasion of a young pregnant woman by a mysterious stranger who seeks to take her unborn baby.  The film received generally positive reviews from mainstream critics upon its release and was particularly well received among horror film critics, noting it for being a genuinely scary and brutally violent example of the new wave of French horror.

Voice (film) | Voice ( also known as Whispering Corridors 4: Voice and Voice Letter) is a 2005 South Korean horror film, and the fourth installment of the "Whispering Corridors" film series.  This film was the debut film for its three young actresses, as well as director Choi Ik-Hwan, who had served as an assistant director on the first film of the series.
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: Eric Red and the (ambiguous) Howard Hughes / similarly named director.

- Eric Red: passage [1] — "Eric Red (born Eric Joseph Durdaller; February 16, 1961) is an American screenwriter and director, best known for writing the horror films 'The Hitcher' and 'Near Dark', as well as writing and directing 'Cohen and Tate'."
- Howard Deutch (different name in passages): passage [3] — "Howard Deutch ... is an American film and television director" and "perhaps best known for his collaboration with filmmaker John Hughes."
- Missing / ambiguous facts: The passages do NOT contain a page or facts for "Howard Hughes" (the name in the question) — candidate targets: "Howard Hughes" (ambiguous / missing) vs "Howard Deutch" (present). The direct comparison of "who was more well-known for horror films" is not present; the bridge entity needed is the correct "Howard Hughes" film director page (or confirmation that the user meant Howard Deutch).
- Next-hop retrieval directives (priority order):
  1. Fetch "Howard Hughes" Wikipedia page (lede/infobox and "Career" or "Filmography" sections) — search terms: "Howard Hughes film director", "Howard Hughes Hell's Angels".
  2. Fetch "Howard Deutch" Wikipedia page (lede/infobox and "Career"/"Filmography" sections) to confirm genre focus (passage [3] may be the intended person).
  3. Fetch "Eric Red" Wikipedia page (lede/infobox and "Career"/"Filmography") to confirm horror credits (verify quotes from passage [1]).
  4. If needed, search: "Howard Hughes horror films" and "Howard Hughes director horror" to find specific horror credits or lack thereof.
hop2_docs: The Outlaw | The Outlaw is a 1943 American Western film, directed by Howard Hughes and starring Jack Buetel, Jane Russell, Thomas Mitchell, and Walter Huston.  Hughes also produced the film, while Howard Hawks served as an uncredited co-director.  The film is notable as Russell's breakthrough role, turning the young actress into a sex symbol and a Hollywood icon.  Later advertising billed Russell as the sole star.

Rupert Hughes | Rupert Hughes (January 31, 1872 – September 9, 1956) was an American novelist, film director, Oscar-nominated screenwriter, military officer, and music composer.  He was the brother of Howard R. Hughes, Sr. and uncle of billionaire Howard R. Hughes, Jr. His three-volume scholarly biography of George Washington broke new ground in demythologizing Washington and was well received by historians.  A staunch anti-Communist, in the 1940s he served as president of the American Writers Association, a group of anti-Communist writers.

The Amazing Howard Hughes | The Amazing Howard Hughes is a 1977 American made-for-television biographical film about American aviation pioneer and filmmaker Howard Hughes, based on the book "Howard: The Amazing Mr. Hughes" by Hughes' business partner Noah Dietrich.  The film starred Tommy Lee Jones, Ed Flanders, and Tovah Feldshuh and was made a year after Hughes's death, in 1976, while aboard a private flight to Mexico.  It was originally broadcast in two parts on CBS on April 13 and 14, 1977.

Howard Deutch | Howard Deutch (born September 14, 1950) is an American film and television director.  He is perhaps best known for his collaboration with filmmaker John Hughes, having directed two of Hughes' well-received screenplays, "Pretty in Pink" and "Some Kind of Wonderful".  Since 2010, he has primarily directed television productions, including multiple episodes of "Getting On" and "True Blood".

Howard Hughes | Howard Robard Hughes Jr. (December 24, 1905 – April 5, 1976) was an American businessman, investor, pilot, film director, and philanthropist, known during his lifetime as one of the most financially successful individuals in the world.  He first made a name for himself as a film producer, and then became an influential figure in the aviation industry.  Later in life, he became known for his eccentric behavior and reclusive lifestyle—oddities that were caused in part by a worsening obsessive–compulsive disorder (OCD) and chronic pain from a plane crash.

Macao (film) | Macao is a 1952 black-and-white film noir adventure directed by Josef von Sternberg and Nicholas Ray.  Producer Howard Hughes fired director von Sternberg during filming and hired Nicholas Ray to finish it.  The drama features Robert Mitchum, Jane Russell, William Bendix, and Gloria Grahame.

Frank William Gay | Frank William "Bill" Gay (September 11, 1920 – May 21, 2007) was an American executive who oversaw several entities for Howard Hughes.  He was chairman of the board of directors of the Hughes Air Corporation.  He served as a senior vice president and member of the board of directors for the Hughes Tool Company.  He was also president and chief executive officer of Summa Corporation.
response.summary_2: Eric Red (born Eric Joseph Durdaller; February 16, 1961) is an American screenwriter and director, best known for writing the horror films 'The Hitcher' and 'Near Dark', as well as writing and directing 'Cohen and Tate'. Howard Hughes was primarily an American businessman, film producer and aviator, and the provided passages do not identify him as being known for horror films.
